name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
نٓ ۚ وَٱلْقَلَمِ وَمَا يَسْطُرُونَ (١)
```

```english
Nun. By the Pen and what they write: (1)
```

```arabic
مَآ أَنتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِمَجْنُونٍ (٢)
```

```english
by your Lord’s blessing, you are not, crazy, (2)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ لَكَ لَأَجْرًا غَيْرَ مَمْنُونٍ (٣)
```

```english
and yours indeed will be an everlasting reward, (3)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلَىٰ خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ (٤)
```

```english
and indeed you possess a great character. (4)
```

```arabic
فَسَتُبْصِرُ وَيُبْصِرُونَ (٥)
```

```english
You will see and they will see, (5)
```

```arabic
بِأَييِّكُمُ ٱلْمَفْتُونُ (٦)
```

```english
which one of you is crazy. (6)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (٧)
```

```english
Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are guided. (7)
```

```arabic
فَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٨)
```

```english
So do not obey the deniers, (8)
```

```arabic
وَدُّوا۟ لَوْ تُدْهِنُ فَيُدْهِنُونَ (٩)
```

```english
who are eager that you should be flexible, so that they [too] may be flexible [towards you]. (9)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تُطِعْ كُلَّ حَلَّافٍ مَّهِينٍ (١٠)
```

```english
And do not obey any vile swearer, (10)
```

```arabic
هَمَّازٍ مَّشَّآءٍۭ بِنَمِيمٍ (١١)
```

```english
scandal-monger, talebearer, (11)
```

```arabic
مَّنَّاعٍ لِّلْخَيْرِ مُعْتَدٍ أَثِيمٍ (١٢)
```

```english
hinderer of all good, sinful transgressor, (12)
```

```arabic
عُتُلٍّۭ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ زَنِيمٍ (١٣)
```

```english
callous and, on top of that, baseborn (13)
```

```arabic
أَن كَانَ ذَا مَالٍ وَبَنِينَ (١٤)
```

```english
—[who behaves in such a manner only] because he has wealth and children. (14)
```

```arabic
إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَـٰتُنَا قَالَ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٥)
```

```english
When Our signs are recited to him, he says, ‘Myths of the ancients!’ (15)
```

```arabic
سَنَسِمُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلْخُرْطُومِ (١٦)
```

```english
Soon We shall brand him on his snout. (16)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا بَلَوْنَـٰهُمْ كَمَا بَلَوْنَآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ إِذْ أَقْسَمُوا۟ لَيَصْرِمُنَّهَا مُصْبِحِينَ (١٧)
```

```english
Indeed we have tested them just as We tested the People of the Garden when they vowed they would gather its fruit at dawn, (17)
```

```arabic
وَلَا يَسْتَثْنُونَ (١٨)
```

```english
and they did not make any exception. (18)
```

```arabic
فَطَافَ عَلَيْهَا طَآئِفٌ مِّن رَّبِّكَ وَهُمْ نَآئِمُونَ (١٩)
```

```english
Then, a visitation from your Lord visited it while they were asleep. (19)
```

```arabic
فَأَصْبَحَتْ كَٱلصَّرِيمِ (٢٠)
```

```english
So, by dawn it was like a harvested field. (20)
```

```arabic
فَتَنَادَوْا۟ مُصْبِحِينَ (٢١)
```

```english
At dawn they called out to one another, (21)
```

```arabic
أَنِ ٱغْدُوا۟ عَلَىٰ حَرْثِكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰرِمِينَ (٢٢)
```

```english
‘Get off early to your field if you have to gather [the fruits].’ (22)
```

```arabic
فَٱنطَلَقُوا۟ وَهُمْ يَتَخَـٰفَتُونَ (٢٣)
```

```english
So off they went, murmuring to one another: (23)
```

```arabic
أَن لَّا يَدْخُلَنَّهَا ٱلْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكُم مِّسْكِينٌ (٢٤)
```

```english
‘Today no needy man shall come to you in it.’ (24)
```

```arabic
وَغَدَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ حَرْدٍ قَـٰدِرِينَ (٢٥)
```

```english
And they set out at early morning, [considering themselves] able to deprive [the poor of its fruit]. (25)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهَا قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا لَضَآلُّونَ (٢٦)
```

```english
But when they saw it, they said, ‘We have indeed lost our way!’ (26)
```

```arabic
بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ (٢٧)
```

```english
‘No, it is we who have been deprived!’ (27)
```

```arabic
قَالَ أَوْسَطُهُمْ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ لَوْلَا تُسَبِّحُونَ (٢٨)
```

```english
The most upright among them said, ‘Did I not tell you, ‘‘Why do you not glorify [Allah]?’’ ’ (28)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَ رَبِّنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٢٩)
```

```english
They said, ‘Immaculate is our Lord! We have indeed been wrongdoers!’ (29)
```

```arabic
فَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ يَتَلَـٰوَمُونَ (٣٠)
```

```english
Then they turned to one another, blaming each other. (30)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا طَـٰغِينَ (٣١)
```

```english
They said, ‘Woe to us! Indeed, we have been rebellious. (31)
```

```arabic
عَسَىٰ رَبُّنَآ أَن يُبْدِلَنَا خَيْرًا مِّنْهَآ إِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا رَٰغِبُونَ (٣٢)
```

```english
Maybe our Lord will give us a better one in its place. Indeed we earnestly beseech our Lord.’ (32)
```

```arabic
كَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْعَذَابُ ۖ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٣)
```

```english
Such was their punishment; and the punishment of the Hereafter is surely greater, had they known. (33)
```

## (Commentary)

This Surah consoles the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) following the polytheists' accusations of madness against him. It comforts his soul with a beautiful promise and praise for his magnificent character, while sternly forbidding him from obeying or compromising with them, and strictly commanding him to be patient with the decree of his Lord.

The context of its verses is generally Meccan. It is narrated from Ibn Abbas and Qatadah that its beginning up to His saying: **سَنَسِمُهُ عَلَى الْخُرْطُومِ** (We shall brand him on the snout)—sixteen verses—is Meccan; what follows until His saying: **لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ** (had they known)—seventeen verses—is Medinan; what follows until His saying: **يَكْتُبُونَ** (they write)—fifteen verses—is Meccan; and from there to the end of the Surah—four verses—is Medinan.

However, there is some plausibility regarding the seventeen verses starting from **إِنَّا بَلَوْناهُمْ** (Indeed We have tried them) until **لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ** (had they known), as they resemble Medinan verses more than Meccan ones.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ن** (Nun) — The discussion regarding the disjointed letters (الحروف المقطعة) at the beginning of the Surahs has already been presented in the commentary on Surah al-Shura.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالْقَلَمِ وَما يَسْطُرُونَ** (By the Pen and what they write) — The pen (القلم) is well-known. The term **سطر** (row/line) refers to a line of writing, a row of planted trees, or a rank of standing people. To "line" (سطر) something means to write it line by line.

The Glorified swore by the pen and what they write with it. The apparent context suggests that what is meant is the pen in an absolute sense and whatever is written in an absolute sense—which is the written word. Indeed, the pen and the writing produced by it are among the greatest divine blessings (النعم الإلهية) to which man has been guided. It follows speech in recording events absent from sight and meanings hidden within the conscience. Through it, it becomes possible for man to summon everything that the passage of time or distance of place has veiled.

Allah, the Glorified, has bestowed His favor upon man by guiding him to these two and teaching them to him. He said regarding speech: **خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ عَلَّمَهُ الْبَيانَ** — "He created man, (4) and taught him articulate speech (البيان) — *the ability to express thoughts and feelings clearly through language*" (al-Rahman: 4). And He said regarding the pen: **عَلَّمَ بِالْقَلَمِ عَلَّمَ الْإِنْسانَ ما لَمْ يَعْلَمْ** — "who taught by the pen, (4) [and] taught man what he did not know" (al-Alaq: 4-5).

Thus, His swearing by the pen and what they write is an oath by a blessing. Allah has frequently sworn in His speech by many of His creations insofar as they are a mercy and a blessing, such as the heaven, the earth, the sun, the moon, the night, and the day, among others—even the fig and the olive.

It has been said that **ما** in His saying **وَما يَسْطُرُونَ** is an infinitive particle (مصدرية), meaning "and [by] the writing."

It has also been said that the "Pen" refers to the Supreme Pen (القلم الأعلى) which, according to the hadith, was the first thing Allah created, and "what they write" refers to what is recorded by the noble guardian scribes. It was also suggested that the plural in **يَسْطُرُونَ** (they write) is for glorification rather than multiplicity, but this is weak. Others suggested it refers to what they write *upon*, namely the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), or that it refers to the possessors of the pen and their writings. These are all weak possibilities.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما أَنْتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِمَجْنُونٍ** — "you are not, by your Lord’s blessing, crazy" — This is the subject of the oath, addressed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The particle (ب) in **بِنِعْمَةِ** denotes causality or accompaniment; i.e., you are not crazy *because of* the blessing—or *along with* the blessing—that your Lord has bestowed upon you.

The context supports that this blessing (النعمة) refers to prophethood (النبوة), for the evidence of prophethood repels all mental deficiency from the Prophet so that the divine guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — necessary for the system of human life may remain upright. The verse refutes the accusation of madness they cast at him, as recounted at the end of the Surah: **وَيَقُولُونَ إِنَّهُ لَمَجْنُونٌ** (and they say, ‘He is indeed crazy’).

It has also been said that the blessing refers to his eloquence (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), his perfect intellect, his pleasing conduct, his innocence from every flaw, and his embodiment of every noble trait. The manifestation of these attributes in him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) negates the occurrence of madness. However, what we previously stated is a more decisive argument. The verse and what follows it are, as you see, a consolation for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), a comfort for his noble soul, and a support for him, just as they contain a refutation of their claims.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّ لَكَ لَأَجْراً غَيْرَ مَمْنُونٍ** — "and indeed for you there will be an unending reward" — The term **الممنون** is derived from **المن** in the sense of cutting off (القطع). It is said: "the journey *mannahu*" when it cuts him off and weakens him. It is not from *minnah* in the sense of verbally reminding someone of a favor to burden them.

The "reward" (الأجر) refers to the reward for the message with Allah, the Glorified. In this is a comfort for the Prophet's soul (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), affirming that for his endurance in delivering Allah's message, he has a reward that will never be cut off and will not go to waste.

Sometimes *mann* is taken to mean the benefactor's mention of his favor to the recipient in a way that burdens him and embitters his life, based on the reasoning that what Allah gives is a reward in exchange for his work, so he deserves it from Allah and thus there is no "favor" (منة) upon him. This is incorrect, for every worker is owned by Allah, the Glorified, in the true sense of ownership (الملك)—in his essence, attributes, and actions. Whatever the servant gives from that is a gift and a grant, and whatever the servant "owns" of it, he only owns because Allah made him the owner. He is the Owner of what He granted before and after. Thus, it is a grace (تفضل) from Him, the Exalted. Even if He calls what He gives in exchange for work a "reward" (أجر) and calls the exchange between Him and His servant a "transaction" (معاملة), that itself is another grace. Therefore, to Allah, the Glorified, belongs the favor (المنة) over all His creation, and the Messenger and those below him are equal in this regard.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلى خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ** — "and indeed you possess a great character" — Character (الخلق) is the psychological disposition (الملكة النفسانية) from which actions proceed with ease. It is divided into virtue (الفضيلة)—which is praised, like temperance and courage—and vice (الرذيلة)—which is censured, like greed and cowardice. However, when used without qualification, it is understood as good character.

Al-Raghib said: "**الخلق** (with *fatha* on the *kha*) and **الخلق** (with *damma* on the *kha*) were originally the same, like *sharb* and *shurb*... but **الخلق** (with *fatha*) was specialized for outward forms and shapes perceived by sight, while **الخلق** (with *damma*) was specialized for the internal powers and dispositions perceived by insight (البصيرة). Allah said: **وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلى خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ**." End quote.

Although the verse in itself praises and magnifies his good character (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), considering the specific context, it looks toward his beautiful social ethics related to interaction, such as steadfastness upon the truth, patience with people's harm and the coarseness of the uncivilized, forgiveness, overlooking faults, immense generosity, gentleness, diplomacy, humility, and so on. We have cited in the end of the sixth volume of this book the narrations concerning the sum of his character (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

From what has preceded, it becomes clear that the view stating "character" here means the religion (الدين)—namely Islam—is not upright except by referring back to what we have mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَسَتُبْصِرُ وَيُبْصِرُونَ بِأَيِّكُمُ الْمَفْتُونُ** — "Soon you will see, and they will see, (5) which of you is the one who is possessed" — This is a rebuke based on the sum of what preceded. That is: if you are not crazy, but rather invested with prophethood and characterized by great character, and you have a great reward from your Lord, then the matter of your mission will soon become manifest. It will be revealed to all eyes and insights who is the one afflicted (المفتون) with madness—you, or the deniers who accuse you of madness?

It has been said: it refers to the manifestation of the outcome of the mission for him and for them in this world or the hereafter. The verse can accommodate both meanings, and there is no barrier to combining them. Indeed, Allah made His Prophet prevail over them and His religion over theirs; He raised his mention (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and erased their trace in this world. They shall taste the evil consequence of their affair tomorrow and they shall know that Allah is the Manifest Truth on the day they are tried (يفتنون) over the Fire: "Taste your trial! This is what you used to hasten."

In His saying: **بِأَيِّكُمُ الْمَفْتُونُ**, the particle (ب) is extra for connection (صلة). **المفتون** is a passive participle from *fitnah* in the sense of affliction (الابتلاء), meaning the one afflicted with madness and loss of intellect. The meaning is: "Soon you will see and they will see which of you is the *maftun*—the one afflicted with madness? You or they?"

It has also been said that **المفتون** is an infinitive (مصدر) on the pattern of *maf'ul*, like *ma'qul* (intellect) and *maysur* (ease). The (ب) in **بِأَيِّكُمُ** would mean "in," and the meaning would be: "Soon you will see and they will see in which of the two groups is the madness (الفتنة)."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ ضَلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُهْتَدِينَ** — "Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are guided" — Since the preceding words implied that there is misguidance and guidance, and indicated that those who accuse the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) of madness are the afflicted ones who are astray—and their state will soon be manifest—while the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is guided, Allah emphasized this by stating that He knows best who has strayed from His path and who is guided. This is because the path is His path, and He knows best who is upon it and who is not, and to Him belongs the matter of guidance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلا تُطِعِ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ** — "So do not obey the deniers" — This is a derivation from the conclusion of the previous verses. "The deniers" (المكذبون) carries a specific reference (العهد). "Obedience" (الطاعة) here means any form of agreement in action or speech. The meaning is: since these deniers of yours are afflicted and astray, do not obey them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَدُّوا لَوْ تُدْهِنُ فَيُدْهِنُونَ** — "They wish that you were soft [with them], so that they [too] would be soft [with you]" — **الإدهان** (softness/compromise) is derived from *duhn* (oil), meaning softening. That is: these deniers wished and loved that you would soften toward them by compromising in your religion, so they would soften toward you by compromising in theirs. The gist is that they wished you would reconcile with each other on the basis that each party tolerates some aspects of the other's religion. It is said they offered that he stop mentioning their gods, and they would stop bothering him and his Lord.

From what has preceded, it is clear that the object of their wish is the entirety of "that you were soft, so they would be soft," and that the *fa* in **فَيُدْهِنُونَ** is for derivation (التفريع), not causality (السببية).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا تُطِعْ كُلَّ حَلَّافٍ مَهِينٍ** — "And do not obey any habitual swearer, mean" — until His saying — **زَنِيمٍ** (ignoble). **الحلاف** is one who swears oaths excessively. The consequence of excessive swearing in every trivial or significant matter, whether true or false, is that the swearer does not respect what he swears by. If his oath is by Allah, he does not sense the greatness of Allah, exalted is His Name—and this is vice enough.

**المهين** (mean/base) is from *mahanah*, meaning insignificance (الحقارة). It refers to insignificance of opinion. It has also been said it means one who abounds in evil, or a liar.

**الهماز** (slanderer) is an intensive form of *hamz*, meaning one who finds fault and defames. It is said to be one who defames with the eye and gestures, or a frequent backbiter.

**المشاء بنميم** (monger of tales) — **النميم** is malicious gossip and sowing discord. The one who "walks with it" is the one who carries talk from one group to another to cause corruption between them.

**المناع للخير** (hinderer of good) is one who frequently prevents the doing of good or prevents the good that reaches its people.

**المعتدي** (transgressor) is from *i'tida'*, which is overstepping the limit through injustice (الظلم).

**الأثيم** (sinful) is one whose sin (إثم) has become so frequent that it has settled within him without departing. Sin is the evil deed that slows down good.

**العتل** (with two *dammas*) is the coarse, thick-natured person. It has been explained as the obscene, ill-mannered person; the harsh one who is fierce in falsehood; the glutton who withholds from others; and the one who drags (يعتل) people to prison or punishment.

**الزنيم** (ignoble/base-born) is one who has no [legitimate] root. It is said to be the pretender (الدعي) attached to a people to whom he does not belong. It is also said to be one known for meanness, or one who has a mark in evil by which he is known, such that when evil is mentioned, he is the first to come to mind. These meanings are close to one another.

These are nine vicious traits with which Allah described some of the enemies of the religion who were calling the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to obedience and compromise. They are the sum of all vices.

His saying: **عُتُلٍّ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ زَنِيمٍ** (coarse, and moreover ignoble) means that after all the flaws and vices mentioned, he is coarse and ignoble. It is said this indicates that these two vices are his most severe defects.

Apparently, it indicates that he possesses such foul attributes that he should not be obeyed in the matter of truth, even if one were to overlook those attributes; for he is coarse, harsh-natured, and rootless. Such a person should not be given weight in human society; he should be rejected and not obeyed in word nor followed in deed.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَنْ كانَ ذا مالٍ وَبَنِينَ** — "[just] because he is a possessor of wealth and sons" — Apparently, it assumes a latent "because" (لام التعليل) and is connected to a verb derived from the sum of the mentioned vicious traits. That is: he does such and such *because* he possesses wealth and sons, so he became insolent thereby, disbelieved in Allah's blessing, and clothed himself in every foul vice instead of thanking Allah for His blessing and reforming himself. Thus, the verse, in conveying censure and sarcasm, follows the course of His saying: "Have you not regarded him who argued with Abraham about his Lord, because Allah had given him kingdom?"

It has also been said it is connected to His previous saying **لا تُطِعْ** (do not obey). The meaning would be: "Do not obey him *because* he possesses wealth and sons"; i.e., do not let his possession of wealth and sons prompt you to obey him. The first meaning is closer and broader.

It is said that it cannot be connected to His saying **قالَ** (he says) in the following conditional clause, because according to grammarians, what follows a condition cannot operate on what precedes it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِ آياتُنا قالَ أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** — "When Our signs are recited to him, he says, ‘Myths of the ancients!’" — **الأساطير** is the plural of *usturah*, which is a fairy tale. The verse serves as a justification for His previous saying: "Do not obey."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سَنَسِمُهُ عَلَى الْخُرْطُومِ** — "Soon We shall brand him on the snout" — **الوسم** and **السمة** mean to place a mark. **الخرطوم** is the nose. It is said that applying the term "snout" to his nose—which is normally used for elephants and pigs—is for sarcasm. The verse contains a threat for his intense enmity toward Allah, His Messenger, and what He revealed to His Messenger.

Apparently, branding on the nose is intended to signify the ultimate humiliation through a visible disgrace by which everyone who sees him will recognize him. This is because the nose is where pride and humiliation are manifest, as it is said: "he raised his nose" (in pride), "he protected his nose," "his nose was rubbed in the dust," and "his nose was cut off."

It appears that the branding on the snout is something that will occur on the Day of Resurrection, not in this world, though some have strained to interpret it as his public disgrace in this world.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا بَلَوْناهُمْ كَما بَلَوْنا أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ** — "Indeed We have tried them just as We tried the owners of the garden" — until His saying — **كَالصَّرِيمِ** (like a harvested patch). **البلاء** is testing (الاختبار) or the striking of a calamity. **الصرم** is the cutting of fruits from trees. **الاستثناء** (exception) is the exclusion of a part from the ruling of the whole; it also refers to saying "if Allah wills" (إن شاء الله) when making a firm statement. This is because its root is exception; the root of your saying "I will go out tomorrow, if Allah wills" is "I will go out tomorrow except if Allah wills that I do not go out." **الطائف** is the punishment that comes by night. **الصريم** is the tree whose fruit has been cut. It is also said to mean the black night, or the sand that is cut off from other sand, which grows nothing and provides no benefit.

These verses—meaning His saying: **إِنَّا بَلَوْناهُمْ كَما بَلَوْنا أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ** until the end of the seventeen verses—are a threat to the deniers of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) who accused him of madness. In the comparison and analogy is an indication that these deniers are inevitably to be punished, and the punishment destined for them is already underway, though they are heedless and will soon know. Today, they are obsessed with amassing wealth and increasing sons, acting arrogantly through them and relying upon them and other outward causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) that suit them and follow their whims, without thanking their Lord for these blessings, following the path of truth, or worshipping their Lord—until their term comes and the punishment of the Hereafter or a worldly punishment from Him surprises them, just as it surprised them on the day of Badr. Then they will see the severance of causes from them, and that wealth and sons are in vain and benefit them nothing, just as the owners of the garden witnessed the like of that regarding their garden. They will regret their conduct and turn toward their Lord, but that will not repel Allah's punishment, just as the owners of the garden regretted, blamed one another, and turned toward their Lord, but that benefited them nothing. "Such is the punishment, and the punishment of the Hereafter is surely greater, had they known." This is assuming the verses are connected to what precedes them and were revealed with them.

As for what has been narrated—that the verses were revealed regarding the famine and drought that struck the people of Mecca and Quraysh following the Prophet's prayer (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) against them: "O Allah, intensify Your grip on Mudar and make it for them years like the years of Joseph"—then the "trial" (البلاء) refers to their being struck by famine. Their story would then parallel the story of the owners of the garden, although the correspondence of what is at the end of their story—"Then they turned to one another, blaming each other"—to the story of the people of Mecca is somewhat obscure.

In any case, the meaning is: **إِنَّا بَلَوْناهُمْ** (Indeed We have tried them)—We struck them with calamity—**كَما بَلَوْنا** (just as We tried)—and struck with calamity—**أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ** (the owners of the garden). They were a people from Yemen and their garden was there; their story will follow, Allah willing, in the upcoming discussion of narrations. **إِذْ** (when) is a temporal adverb for "We tried." **أَقْسَمُوا** (they swore) and took an oath **لَيَصْرِمُنَّها** (that they would surely harvest it)—i.e., cut and pluck the fruits of their garden—**مُصْبِحِينَ** (at daybreak)—entering upon the morning. It is as if they conspired and consulted at night, then resolved to harvest on the morning of their night. **وَلا يَسْتَثْنُونَ** (without making any exception)—they did not say "if Allah wills," relying on themselves and depending on outward causes. Or the meaning is: they spoke while not setting aside (عزل) a portion of their fruits for the poor and needy.

**فَطافَ عَلَيْها** (So there went around it)—around the garden—**طائِفٌ** (a visitor)—i.e., a calamity that goes around it and encompasses it at night—**مِنْ رَبِّكَ** (from your Lord), **فَأَصْبَحَتْ** (and it became)—the garden became—**كَالصَّرِيمِ** (like a harvested patch), which is the tree whose fruit has been cut. Or the meaning is: the garden became like the black night because it was blackened by the burning of the fire that Allah sent to it. Or the meaning is: the garden became like a piece of sand with no vegetation or benefit.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَتَنادَوْا مُصْبِحِينَ** — "Then they called out to one another at daybreak" — until His saying — **قادِرِينَ** (determined). **التنادي** is the calling of some of the people to others. **الإصباح** is entering upon the morning. **صارمين** is from *sarm*, meaning the cutting of fruits from the tree; here it refers to those intending to cut the fruits. **الحرث** is the crop and the trees. **الخفت** is hiding and concealment. **الحرد** is prevention. **قادرين** is from *qadr* in the sense of determination (التقدير).

The meaning is: **فَتَنادَوْا** (Then they called out to one another)—i.e., some of the people called others—**مُصْبِحِينَ** (at daybreak)—i.e., while they were entering the morning—**أَنِ اغْدُوا عَلى حَرْثِكُمْ** (Go early to your tilth). This explains the calling; i.e., go early, heading toward your garden. *Ighdu* is a command meaning "go early," implying "head toward," which is why it is followed by *'ala* (upon/toward). If it did not imply heading toward, it would be followed by *ila* (to), as in *al-Kashshaf*. **إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صارِمِينَ** (if you are going to harvest)—i.e., intending and resolved upon harvesting and cutting.

"So they went off" and went to their garden **وَهُمْ يَتَخافَتُونَ** (whispering to one another)—i.e., while they were conspiring among themselves in a manner of whispering and secrecy—**أَنْ لا يَدْخُلَنَّهَا** (that no [needy person] shall enter it)—i.e., the garden—**الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِسْكِينٌ** (today upon you). That is: hide your arrival at the garden for harvesting from the needy so they do not enter upon you, which would compel you to set aside a portion of the harvested fruit for them. **وَغَدَوْا** (And they went early) to the garden **عَلى حَرْدٍ** (with a purpose)—i.e., with the intent of preventing the needy—**قادِرِينَ** (determined)—calculating in their souls that they would harvest it and not share any of it with the needy.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَمَّا رَأَوْها قالُوا إِنَّا لَضَالُّونَ بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ** — "But when they saw it, they said, ‘We have indeed lost our way! (26) No, we are [ourselves] deprived!’" — i.e., when they saw the garden and witnessed it having become like a harvested patch due to a visitor from Allah, they said: "We have indeed strayed from the right course in our going early to it with the intent of harvesting and preventing the needy."

It has also been said the meaning is: "We have indeed lost the way to our garden, and this is not it."

His saying: **بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ** (No, we are deprived) is a retraction (إضراب) from the previous statement; i.e., it is not merely straying from the right course, but rather we have been deprived of the crop.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ أَوْسَطُهُمْ أَلَمْ أَقُلْ لَكُمْ لَوْ لا تُسَبِّحُونَ** — "The most moderate of them said, ‘Did I not tell you, "Why do you not glorify [Allah]?"’" — until His saying — **راغِبُونَ** (turning).

**قالَ أَوْسَطُهُمْ** (The most moderate of them said)—i.e., the most balanced of them in conduct. This is because he reminded them of the truth even though he followed them in action. It has been said it means the middle one in age, but that is nothing. **أَلَمْ أَقُلْ لَكُمْ** (Did I not tell you?)—and he had indeed told them that, though it was not mentioned earlier in the story for the sake of brevity, relying on its mention here.

**لَوْ لا تُسَبِّحُونَ** (Why do you not glorify?)—The meaning of their glorifying Him, the Exalted, is their declaring Him free from partners (الشركاء), as they had relied upon themselves and other outward causes, swearing they would surely harvest it at daybreak without making an exception for Allah's will. Thus, they isolated Him, the Exalted, from causality and influence, and attributed influence to themselves and other outward causes, which is an affirmation of a partner. Had they said: "We shall surely harvest it at daybreak unless Allah wills," that would have meant the negation of partners and that if they did not harvest, it was due to a will from Allah, and if they did harvest, it was by permission from Allah. For to Allah alone belongs the command, He has no partner.

It has also been said that their glorifying Allah refers to the remembrance of Allah, the Exalted, and their repentance to Him, since they had intended to harvest it and deprive the needy of it. This has some plausibility if "exception" is taken to mean setting aside a portion of the fruits for the needy.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا سُبْحانَ رَبِّنا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ** — "They said, ‘Immaculate is our Lord! We have indeed been wrongdoers.’" — This is a glorification of Allah from them following the rebuke of the most moderate among them. That is: "We declare Allah free from the partners we affirmed in what we swore upon; for He is our Lord who disposes our affairs by His will, because we were wrongdoers in our affirmation of partners." Thus, it is a glorification and an admission of their wrongdoing against themselves in affirming partners.

According to the other view, it is a repentance and an admission of their wrongdoing against themselves and against the needy.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ يَتَلاوَمُونَ** — "Then they turned to one another, blaming each other" — i.e., some of them blamed others for the injustice they had committed.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا يا وَيْلَنا** — "They said, ‘Woe to us!" — until His saying — **راغِبُونَ** (turning). **الطغيان** (rebellion) is overstepping the limit. The pronoun in **مِنْها** (from it) refers to the garden in terms of its fruits. The meaning is: they said, "Woe to us! We were indeed overstepping the limit of servitude (العبودية) when we affirmed partners for our Lord and did not maintain His oneness (التوحيد). We hope our Lord will give us in exchange something better than this garden which a visitor from Him went around, for we are turning toward Him, turning away from all else."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَذلِكَ الْعَذابُ وَلَعَذابُ الْآخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ** — "Such is the punishment! And the punishment of the Hereafter is surely greater, had they known." — "The punishment" (العذاب) is a delayed subject (مبتدأ مؤخر), and "Such" (كذلك) is a preceding predicate (خبر مقدم). That is: the punishment is only as we have described in the story of the owners of the garden. It is that man is tested with wealth and sons, so he rebels, deluded by that, considering himself self-sufficient and forgetting his Lord. He associates partners with outward causes and with himself, and dares to commit sin while heedless of the evil consequence of his deed that encompasses him and the punishment being prepared for him—until the punishment surprises him and appears to him in its most terrifying and bitter forms. Then he wakes from the sleep of heedlessness, remembers the advice that came to him before, regrets what he neglected through rebellion and injustice, and asks Allah to return the blessing to him so he may be thankful—just as the matter of the owners of the garden ended. Thus, this provides a general rule through an example.

His saying: **وَلَعَذابُ الْآخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ** (And the punishment of the Hereafter is surely greater, had they known) — because it arises from a divine subjugation (قهر إلهي) that nothing can withstand. There is no hope of escaping it, even through death or annihilation, as in the hardships of this world. It encompasses man from all sides of his existence, unlike the punishment of this world. It is eternal, with no end to its term, unlike worldly afflictions.

## (Discussion of Narrations)

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain of transmission from Sufyan ibn Sa'id al-Thawri from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding the interpretation of the disjointed letters in the Quran, he said: "As for *Nun*, it is a river in Paradise—Allah (عز وجل) said: 'Freeze!' and it froze and became ink. Then He said to the Pen: 'Write!' and the Pen wrote upon the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) what was and what will be until the Day of Resurrection. So the ink is ink of light, the Pen is a pen of light, and the Tablet is a tablet of light."

Sufyan said: I said to him: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, explain the matter of the Tablet, the Pen, and the Ink with further clarification, and teach me from what Allah has taught you." He said: "O son of Sa'id, were you not worthy of an answer, I would not have answered you. *Nun* is an angel who delivers to the Pen, which is an angel; the Pen delivers to the Tablet, which is an angel; the Tablet delivers to Israfil; Israfil delivers to Mika'il; Mika'il delivers to Gabriel; and Gabriel delivers to the prophets and messengers." He said: Then he said: "Rise, O Sufyan, for I do not feel secure for you [from the weight of this knowledge]."

In the same source, with its chain from Ibrahim al-Karkhi, he said: I asked Ja'far ibn Muhammad (عليه السلام) about the Tablet and the Pen. He said: "They are two angels."

In the same source, with its chain from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام): **ن وَالْقَلَمِ وَما يَسْطُرُونَ** — "The Pen is a pen of light, and a book of light in a preserved tablet, witnessed by those brought near (المقربون), and Allah suffices as a witness."

**I say:** There are other narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) with similar meanings. We have already presented, under His saying: **هذا كِتابُنا يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ** (al-Jathiyah: 29), the hadith of al-Qummi from Abd al-Rahim al-Qasir from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding the Tablet and the Pen, which states: "Then He sealed the mouth of the Pen, so it did not speak after that and will never speak. It is the Hidden Book (الكتاب المكنون) from which all transcriptions (النسخ) are made."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir narrated from Mu'awiyah ibn Qurrah from his father, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: **ن وَالْقَلَمِ وَما يَسْطُرُونَ** — "A tablet of light and a pen of light, recording what will be until the Day of Resurrection."

**I say:** There are other narrations with this meaning. His saying "recording what will be..." means it corresponds to the reality of beings without anything deviating from what was written there. Similar to this is what is in the narration of Abu Hurayrah: "Then the Pen was sealed, so it did not speak and will not speak until the Day of Resurrection."

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain from Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) regarding the saying of Allah (عز وجل): **وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلى خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ** — He said: "It is Islam."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, from Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) regarding His saying: **وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلى خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ** — He said: "Upon a great religion."

**I say:** He means that the religion and Islam encompass the perfection of character and that he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) followed its path. In the well-known narration from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "I was sent only to complete the noble traits of character."

In *al-Majma'*, with its chain from al-Hakim with his chain from al-Dahhak, he said: When Quraysh saw the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) preference for Ali and his magnifying of him, they spoke ill of Ali and said: "Muhammad has become infatuated with him." So Allah, the Exalted, revealed: **ن وَالْقَلَمِ وَما يَسْطُرُونَ** — an oath by which Allah swore — **ما أَنْتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِمَجْنُونٍ ـ وَإِنَّ لَكَ لَأَجْراً غَيْرَ مَمْنُونٍ ـ وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلى خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ** — meaning the Quran — until His saying — **بِمَنْ ضَلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ** — and they are the group who said what they said — **وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُهْتَدِينَ** — meaning Ali ibn Abi Talib.

**I say:** It was also narrated in *Tafsir al-Burhan* from Muhammad ibn al-Abbas with his chain to al-Dahhak, and he cited something similar to what has passed, and at its end: "and His path is Ali ibn Abi Talib."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تُطِعْ كُلَّ حَلَّافٍ** etc., it is said: it means al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah—he offered the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) wealth to turn back from his religion. It is also said: it means al-Akhnas ibn Shariq, from Ata. It is also said: it means al-Aswad ibn Abd Yaghuth, from Mujahid.

**I say:** There are narrations regarding this in *al-Durr al-Manthur* and elsewhere which we have omitted; whoever wishes may refer to the collections of narrations.

In the same source, from Shaddad ibn Aws, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "No *jawwaz*, nor *ja'dhari*, nor *utull zanim* shall enter Paradise." I said: "What is a *jawwaz*?" He said: "Every amasser [of wealth] who is a withholder [of good]." I said: "What is a *ja'dhari*?" He said: "The coarse and harsh one." I said: "What is the *utull zanim*?" He said: "Every wide-bellied one—ill-mannered, a glutton, a heavy drinker, oppressive, unjust, and ignoble (زنيم)."

In the same source, regarding the meaning of *zanim*: It is said: he is the one who has no [legitimate] root.

In the same source, in the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding His saying: **عُتُلٍّ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ زَنِيمٍ**, he said: "The *utull* is the one great in disbelief, and the *zanim* is the pretender (الدعي)."

In the same source, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام): regarding His saying: **إِنَّا بَلَوْناهُمْ كَما بَلَوْنا أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ**, he said: "The people of Mecca were tested with hunger just as the owners of the garden were tested—and it was in this world and was in Yemen—it was called al-Radwan, nine miles from Sana'a."

In the same source, with its chain to Ibn Abbas: It was said to him that a group from this Ummah claim that a servant commits a sin and is thereby deprived of provision. Ibn Abbas said: "By Allah, besides whom there is no god, this is clearer in the Book of Allah than the bright sun—Allah mentioned it in Surah *Nun wal-Qalam*."

"There was an old man who had a garden, and he would not bring a single fruit from it into his house or his residence until he had given every rightful person his due. When the old man passed away, his sons inherited it—and he had five sons. Their garden produced a crop in that year in which their father died such as it had never produced before. The youths went to their garden after the Asr prayer and looked upon a fruit and an abundant provision the like of which they had not seen in their father's lifetime."

"When they saw the abundance, they became insolent and rebellious. Some of them said to others: 'Our father was a very old man; his intellect had gone and he was senile. Come, let us make a pact among ourselves that we will not give anything to any of the poor Muslims in this year of ours, until we become wealthy and our riches increase. Then we shall resume the practice in the coming years.' Four of them agreed to that, while the fifth was displeased—and he is the one of whom Allah said: **قالَ أَوْسَطُهُمْ أَلَمْ أَقُلْ لَكُمْ لَوْ لا تُسَبِّحُونَ**."

"A man said: 'O Ibn Abbas, was he the middle one in age?' He said: 'No, rather he was the youngest in age and the greatest in intellect—and the *awsat* (middle/moderate) of a people is the best of the people. The evidence for this in the Quran is His saying: "Indeed you, O Ummah of Muhammad, are the smallest of nations and the best of nations"—the saying of the Exalted: **وَكَذلِكَ جَعَلْناكُمْ أُمَّةً وَسَطاً** (And thus We have made you a middle nation).'"

"Their most moderate one said to them: 'Fear [Allah] and follow the path of your father; you will be safe and gain.' But they fell upon him and beat him severely. When the brother realized they intended to kill him, he entered into their counsel with them, hating their affair and not being willing."

"They went to their homes and then swore by Allah that they would surely harvest when they reached the morning, and they did not say 'if Allah wills.' So Allah tested them for that sin and stood between them and that provision they had looked upon. He informed about them in the Book, saying: **إِنَّا بَلَوْناهُمْ كَما بَلَوْنا أَصْحابَ الْجَنَّةِ إِذْ أَقْسَمُوا لَيَصْرِمُنَّها مُصْبِحِينَ وَلا يَسْتَثْنُونَ فَطافَ عَلَيْها طائِفٌ مِنْ رَبِّكَ وَهُمْ نائِمُونَ فَأَصْبَحَتْ كَالصَّرِيمِ**. He said: 'Like something burnt.'"

"The man said: 'O Ibn Abbas, what is *al-sarim*?' He said: 'The dark night; it has no light and no glow.'"

"When the people reached the morning: **فَتَنادَوْا مُصْبِحِينَ ـ أَنِ اغْدُوا عَلى حَرْثِكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صارِمِينَ**. He said: **فَانْطَلَقُوا وَهُمْ يَتَخافَتُونَ**. The man said: 'What is *al-takhafut*, O Ibn Abbas?' He said: 'They were consulting, some of them whispering to others so that no one else would hear them. They said: **لا يَدْخُلَنَّهَا الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِسْكِينٌ وَغَدَوْا عَلى حَرْدٍ قادِرِينَ**—in their souls [intending] to harvest it, not knowing what had befallen them of Allah's assaults and His vengeance.'"

"**فَلَمَّا رَأَوْها** (But when they saw it) and what had befallen them, **قالُوا إِنَّا لَضَالُّونَ بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ**. So Allah deprived them of that provision because of a sin they committed—and He did not wrong them in the least."

"**قالَ أَوْسَطُهُمْ أَلَمْ أَقُلْ لَكُمْ لَوْ لا تُسَبِّحُونَ ـ قالُوا سُبْجانَ رَبِّنا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ فَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ يَتَلاوَمُونَ**. He said: 'They were blaming themselves for what they had resolved upon.' **قالُوا يا وَيْلَنا إِنَّا كُنَّا طاغِينَ عَسى رَبُّنا أَنْ يُبْدِلَنا خَيْراً مِنْها إِنَّا إِلى رَبِّنا راغِبُونَ**. Then Allah said: **كَذلِكَ الْعَذابُ وَلَعَذابُ الْآخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ**."

**I say:** Something close to the content of this hadith and the one before it has been reported in other narrations. In some narrations, the garden belonged to a man from the Children of Israel, then he died and his sons inherited it, and their affair was as it was.
